text.title: Problem Validation 2026 Additions
text.summary: Five additions over the base problem-validation methodology: validation hierarchy with numeric ranks, Mom Test replacement table, 5-step opener, commitment signal taxonomy, and recurring loop principle.

rule.statement: Rank evidence 1-5 before synthesizing: 1=paid (users paid for solution — strongest), 2=committed (signed up, LOI, pre-order), 3=engaged (used prototype deeply, returned unprompted), 4=stated (expressed interest in interview), 5=anecdote (weakest — single mention). Only ranks 1-3 support PROCEED.
rule.rationale: Explicit numeric ranking prevents post-hoc rationalization; a researcher who collected only rank-4 and rank-5 evidence cannot argue they have validation without contradicting the hierarchy.

rule.statement: Replace these question patterns before every interview: "Would you use this?" becomes "How do you currently solve X?"; "Is this a problem for you?" becomes "Tell me about the last time you dealt with X"; "Would you pay $X?" becomes "What have you tried? What did it cost?"; "Do you think this is a good idea?" becomes "When was the last time you faced this problem?"
rule.rationale: Hypothetical questions produce socially desirable yes-answers regardless of actual intent. Each replacement anchors to a concrete past event the interviewee experienced.

rule.statement: Open every validation interview with: (1) Vision — state the problem you want to solve in one sentence; (2) Framing — explain you have nothing to sell, just learning; (3) Weakness — humbly acknowledge you don't know if this matters; (4) Pedestal — ask for their expertise on the topic; (5) Ask — request 20 minutes of their time.
rule.rationale: The opener delays any hint of a sales pitch until trust is established; positioning the researcher as a humble learner rather than a pitching founder reduces social desirability bias in answers.

rule.statement: After each interview, classify evidence by signal type: Time (scheduled follow-up or used prototype unprompted), Reputation (offered introduction to colleague or boss), Money (pre-order, LOI, or payment before product exists). Absence of all three = stated interest only — insufficient for PROCEED.
rule.rationale: Commitment signals require the user to give up something real (time, social capital, money). Stated interest costs nothing and therefore predicts nothing about actual adoption.

rule.statement: Model validation as a recurring weekly task during discovery, not a one-time gate. Each round produces a hypothesis; repeated rounds produce confidence. Schedule validation cadence before the first interview, not after.
rule.rationale: Single-round validation produces a snapshot; the market, the problem definition, and the target segment all shift during discovery. Weekly rounds catch these shifts before they invalidate assumptions built on stale data.

rule.statement: Treat compliments, hypotheticals, generic statements, and approval-fishing responses as negative signals that require additional rounds, not as confirmation. A single "would you use this?" answer of "yes" is insufficient regardless of enthusiasm.
rule.rationale: Interviewees optimize for the researcher's comfort; enthusiasm in an interview predicts politeness, not purchase behavior.
